coredata.dc:title: Influence of nanofillers (Ag NPs and C. dots) on the controlled drug release profile of gelatin-grafted-polyacrylamide hydrogel: An in vitro study
coredata.dc:creator: Tanwar, Archana
Kalode, Pratik
V, Roshni
B.K., Prema
Doshi, Pooja
Ottoor, Divya
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogel nanocomposites, one of the essential biomaterials, because of its enhanced mechanical strength, improved swelling properties and stimuli responsiveness has recently become quite popular in the domains of drug delivery, tissue engineering, and biomedicine. In this work, silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) / carbon dots (C. dots) embedded gelatin-grafted polyacrylamide hydrogel nanocomposites were fabricated to understand their role in drug release, a characteristics feature of the hydrogel system. Different parameters such as swelling, thermal stability, biodegradation and antibacterial properties were adopted in order to investigate the effect of incorporation of the nanofillers on pre-synthesized hydrogel. The swelling properties and thermal stability of the hydrogel were significantly improved due to the presence of nanofillers. The in vitro release of ciprofloxacin (CFX) was performed from these nanocomposite systems at different pH conditions, i.e. pH 1.2, pH 6.8 and pH 7.4. CFX release was found to be more at pH 7.4 as compared to pH 1.2. As compared to bare hydrogel, hydrogel nanocomposite systems have demonstrated more controlled release of CFX, thus making them an appealing delivery system candidate. Antibacterial studies on E. coli and S. aureus revealed that incorporation of Ag NPs imparted antibacterial property to the hydrogel system. C. dots enhanced the hydrophilicity of the system, also, it has great potential to be employed as bioimaging agent to track the drug release process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogel nanocomposites
Nanofillers
Silver nanoparticles
Carbon dots
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of gelatin grafted polyacrylamide Ag NPs hydrogel nanocomposite (GAmAg)
Synthesis of C. dots incorporated gelatin grafted polyacrylamide hydrogel nanocomposite (GAmC)
Drug loading (% DL) and drug entrapment (% DE)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the development of Ag NPs in the swollen GAm hydrogel matrix.
Schematic illustration of the formation C. dots embedded GAm hydrogel nanocomposite.

a) UV–Vis absorbance spectra confirming the formation of Ag NPs in the swollen hydrogel matrix, b) UV–visible absorbance and emission spectra of C. dots (inset: image of C. dots in daylight (left) and under UV-light at 365 nm (right)).
a) FTIR spectra of Gel B, GAm and GAmAg and b) FTIR spectra of C. dots and GAmC.
SEM images of a) GAm, b) GAmAg with Ag NPs, c) C. dots in GAm observed at 500 nm scale and d) C. dots in GAm observed at 400 nm scale.

Effect of concentration of a) AgNO3 on % ESR, b) Effect of C.dot on % ESR under different pH conditions studied at 37 ± 2 °C, where C1, C2 and C3 represent the hydrogel systems containing 0.2, 0.6 and 1.0 mg mL−1 C. dots.

Effect of concentration of a) AgNO3 used for synthesis of Ag NPs on % CDR under different pH conditions studied at 37 ± 2 °C, where A1, A2 and A3 are the three systems containing Ag NPs prepared using 5, 10 and 15 mM AgNO3 respectively, b) C. dots on % % CDR from systems containing 0.2, 0.6 and 1.0 mg mL−1 C. dots (C1, C2 and C3 respectively).
Antibacterial study of a) GAmAg and b) GAmC against gram positive S. aureus and gram-negative E. coli bacteria.

head.ce:title: Influence of nanofillers (Ag NPs and C. dots) on the controlled drug release profile of gelatin-grafted-polyacrylamide hydrogel: An in vitro study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Bharti Vidyapeeth (Deemed to be University), College of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Bharti Vidyapeeth (Deemed to be University), College of Engineering, Pune 411043

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogel nanocomposites, one of the essential biomaterials, because of its enhanced mechanical strength, improved swelling properties and stimuli responsiveness has recently become quite popular in the domains of drug delivery, tissue engineering, and biomedicine. In this work, silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) / carbon dots (C. dots) embedded gelatin-grafted polyacrylamide hydrogel nanocomposites were fabricated to understand their role in drug release, a characteristics feature of the hydrogel system. Different parameters such as swelling, thermal stability, biodegradation and antibacterial properties were adopted in order to investigate the effect of incorporation of the nanofillers on pre-synthesized hydrogel. The swelling properties and thermal stability of the hydrogel were significantly improved due to the presence of nanofillers. The in vitro release of ciprofloxacin (CFX) was performed from these nanocomposite systems at different pH conditions, i.e. pH 1.2, pH 6.8 and pH 7.4. CFX release was found to be more at pH 7.4 as compared to pH 1.2. As compared to bare hydrogel, hydrogel nanocomposite systems have demonstrated more controlled release of CFX, thus making them an appealing delivery system candidate. Antibacterial studies on E. coli and S. aureus revealed that incorporation of Ag NPs imparted antibacterial property to the hydrogel system. C. dots enhanced the hydrophilicity of the system, also, it has great potential to be employed as bioimaging agent to track the drug release process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Controlled drug delivery systems can improve the therapeutic efficacy and safety of drug molecules. An appropriate drug delivery system is characterized by its ability to deliver optimum concentration of drug for a longer period with improved bioavailability and, consequently, better therapeutic efficacy. In order to achieve an effective biocompatible delivery system, liposomes, nanoparticles, hydrogels, micelles, dendrimers, and mesoporous particles have been successfully developed [1]. Among these, hydrogels are gaining immense popularity in biomedical fields, with various applications such as drug administration, wound dressings, biosensors, tissue engineering, soft actuators and separation systems. This is because of their remarkable properties including hydrophilicity, softness, swelling ability, flexibility, and biocompatibility [2]. Hydrogels are three-dimensional, physically or chemically crosslinked polymeric networks having the ability to hold large amounts of fluids (water, organic solvents or biological fluids). However, due to fragile inherent structure, traditional hydrogels show poor mechanical performance and toughness, which inhibits their possible applications in artificial cartilage, artificial muscle, wound dressings and drug delivery [3]. Therefore, many attempts have been made to develop tough hydrogels and double network hydrogels, among which hydrogel nanocomposites are of great attraction due to their simple and adaptable synthesis procedures [4–6]. Hydrogel nanocomposites are hydrogel networks that embrace nanoparticles within their matrix. In addition to the biocompatible properties of the original hydrogel, they also display multifunctionality and enhanced mechanical strength [7]. A recent review by Mamidi et.al., explores the present and future possibilities of nanomaterial incorporated biomaterials. There are several nanomaterials which can be embedded within the hydrogels, such as carbon based nano-materials, metal nanoparticles, metal oxide nanoparticles, organic particulate fillers, polymeric nanomaterials and resins [8]. In the past few decades, research work based on hydrogel nanocomposites and its myriad applications in biomedical and tissue engineering areas have been progressed [2]. The unique optical, electronic and catalytic properties of nanoparticles have attracted many researchers to employ them in several fields such as photonics, electronics, drug delivery, chemical sensing, imaging, environmental remediation and biological labeling [9,10]. However, safety related to the applications of nanoparticles in biomedical areas still remains as a matter of concern. Incorporation of nanoparticles within hydrogels not only offers improved properties, and makes hydrogel applicable in a wider range of fields, but also helps in minimizing the toxicity of nanoparticles in humans and environment [11]. Incorporating nanoparticles into the hydrogel matrix improves the shortcomings of hydrogels by imparting novel chemical, biological, physical, and electrical properties [12]. Also, hydrogels not only accommodate the nanoparticles within its matrix but can also act as a nanoreactor for the synthesis of nanoparticles within the hydrogel matrix [11]. Here, hydrogel matrix acts as a “host” which occupies different types of nanoparticles as “guest” and forms a hydrogel nanocomposite. The development of such hydrogels with tailored functions has opened up a new avenue for creating novel materials for biomedical applications. Recent studies on generation of metal nanoparticles within the hydrogel matrix have suggested that hydrogels provide a large free space between their crosslinked network. Hydrogels in the swollen form can act as nanoreactors for the in-situ development of nanoparticles [13]. Also, metal nanoparticles can have remarkable influence on the properties of hydrogels such as hydrophilicity, swelling behavior and mechanical strength which have not been entirely explored. Ag NPs have been utilized in hydrogels as they exhibit significant influence in properties such as swelling/deswelling, mechanical toughness, electrical conductivity, optical properties, and antimicrobial effects [14]. Currently, different hydrogel nanocomposites incorporated with Ag NPs have been reported. Like, Banerjee et al. have reported the development of new Ag NPs incorporated thermoresponsive hydrogel for wound healing [15]. Prusty et al. prepared Ag NPs incorporated hybrid hydrogel comprising of cellulose, polypropylene oxide/ poly ethylene oxide for in vitro release of ornidazole [16]. In another study, Thomas et al. have reported unique method for the synthesis of silver hydrogel nanocomposite, which comprises of generation of Ag NPs inside the swollen matrix of poly (acrylamide-co-acrylic acid) hydrogels [17]. Porter et al. have developed Ag NPs incorporated alginate hydrogel matrix for antibacterial and antibiofilm applications [18]. Considering these facts, in this study, an attempt is made to synthesize Ag NPs within the swollen matrix of gelatin grafted polyacrylamide hydrogel.

Similarly, carbon dots (C. dots) have recently found interesting applications in bioimaging, photocatalysis, sensors, drug delivery, LEDS, supercapacitors and food science [19,20]. C. dots are reported as excellent bioimaging probes due to their strong fluorescence properties and non-cytotoxicity. Thus, integration of C. dots into the hydrogel matrix can be used as a tracking tool of the release behavior of drugs [21]. Incorporation of C. dots within the hydrogel network, and their applications in sensing, light emitting devices, drug delivery systems etc. have been recently reported in the literature [22]. Wang et al. developed C. dot embedded thermo-sensitive hydrogel system comprising of poloxamer 407 for topical ocular drug delivery [23]. According to the recent literature, application of C. dots incorporated hydrogel in drug delivery has been initiated. For instance, Sachdev et al. developed chitosan-based hydrogel containing carbon dot for anticancer 5- fluorouracil drug delivery [24]. Similarly, Yan et al. prepared chitosan hydrogel incorporated with carbon quantum dots for sensing mercury ions [25]. Also, the recent progresses in synthesis of C. dots, basic strategies involved in development of C. dots/hydrogel nanocomposites and their applications in energy, environment and biomedical field have been summarized by Feng et al. [26]. In another work, Panda et al. have prepared thermosensitive PVA-melamine hydrogel encapsulated with C. dots derived from papaya leaf. This hydrogel system was used as drug carrier for anticancer drug 5-Fluorouracil [27].

Thus, to understand the consequence of nanoparticles on the swelling behavior of gelatin grafted acrylamide hydrogel system as well as the release behavior of CFX from the hydrogel system, nanoparticles incorporated hydrogels nanocomposites containing silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) and carbon dots (C. dots) have been prepared and characterized in this work.

Gelatin type B (Gel) extracted from Bovine skin (pH 4–6) was procured from Sisco Research Laboratory (SRL). Acrylamide (Am) and potassium persulfate (KPS) were obtained from SD Fine chemicals. D-glucose and cystamine dihydrochloride were procured from SRL and Sigma Aldrich respectively. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) and tri sodium citrate (TSC) used were purchased from SRL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of gelatin grafted polyacrylamide Ag NPs hydrogel nanocomposite (GAmAg)
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of gelatin polyacrylamide hydrogel (GAm) was carried out using free radical polymerization method. The detailed procedure for the synthesis has been reported in our previous paper [28]. While, for the synthesis of Ag NPs loaded gelatin polyacrylamide hydrogel nanocomposite, Ag NPs were synthesized in situ using the chemical reduction method [29]. In this, dried GAm hydrogel was equilibrated in water for 24 h and then immersed in AgNO3 solution for another 24 h. After removal of excess AgNO3 solution from the hydrogel with distilled water, it was immersed in a solution of trisodium citrate (TSC) (1.5% w/v) which acts as a reducing agent, for 48 h. The color of the hydrogel changed from pale yellow to dark brown, indicating that Ag+ is reduced to Ag0 and Ag NPs are formed within the matrix.

The synthesis of C. dots was carried out using hydrothermal method [21]. For this, 1.0 g of D-glucose and 1.0 g of cystamine dihydrochloride were dissolved in 30.0 mL distilled water. This solution was transferred to a hydrothermal autoclave and heated in a muffle furnace at 250 ºC for 6 h. After cooling, the solution obtained from autoclave was centrifuged and filtered through Whatmann paper. The filtrate was then dialyzed for 24 h and lyophilized to acquire powdered C. dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C. dots incorporated gelatin grafted polyacrylamide hydrogel nanocomposite (GAmC)
ce:section.ce:para: GAmC is synthesized using free radical polymerization method using KPS as initiator and MBA as crosslinker. In this, 1.5 g gelatin was dissolved in different concentrations of 35.0 mL C. dot solution (0.2, 0.6 and 1.0 mg mL−1). On complete dissolution of gelatin, KPS was added and mixed. After 10 min, acrylamide was added and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, MBA was added as crosslinker and the polymerization reaction was continued for 2 h. The product was cooled and dispersed in alcohol for 12 h to remove unreacted monomer and crosslinker. Finally, the product was transferred to a petri dish and dried in the oven at 60 ºС.

The developed hydrogel nanocomposites containing Ag NPs and C. dots were characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR) (Shimadzu 8400). Thermal studies were carried out using thermogravimetric analysis (Shimadzu TGA 60-H) instrument. The study was conducted at a heating rate of 10 ºC/min under inert atmosphere (N2 flow rate was maintained at 50 mL/min). The morphological studies were carried out using scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Model Nova Nanosem 450) and the size of the C. dots was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) Tecnai G2–20 Twin (FEI, the Netherlands) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. While, in vitro release of ciprofloxacin (CFX) was examined using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 1600).

The experiments were performed in triplicates and standard deviations were calculated. The drug release and release kinetics was determined using linear regression with various mathematical kinetic models like zero order, first order, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas models. The protocols followed for studying equilibrium swelling ratio (% ESR), drug loading (DL), drug entrapment (DE), in vitro drug release study, in vitro biodegradation study, antibacterial studies and release kinetics are conversed in the supporting information (Section S1).

Due to high surface energy, nanoparticles tend to form aggregates due to which they tend to lose their unique properties, thereby limiting their applications in different fields. To avoid the aggregation of nanoparticles, capping agents based on organic compounds, surfactants, and polymers have been reported [30]. Polymers in various forms such as nano fibers, microfibers, sheets, microgels and hydrogels are been used in different instances [31]. Fig. 1 represents the schematic formation of Ag NPs within the swollen GAm matrix which acts as a reservoir for Ag+ ions.

When swollen GAm is immersed in AgNO3 solution Ag+ ions from the outer solution are exchanged for H+ ions present within the hydrogel matrix. The presence of hydrophilic groups (-COOH, -OH) in the hydrogel matrix, allows easy infusion of Ag+ ions, enabling effective immobilization of Ag+ ions. The hydrogel matrix also facilities reduction process and stabilization of the Ag NPs. Ag NPs are generated, when the hydrogel after attaining equilibrium is transferred to aqueous solution of trisodium citrate (TSC) where the Ag+ ions are reduced to Ag0. The Ag NPs produced would acquire a negative charge on their surface due to presence of citrate ions. These negatively charged Ag NPs would have interactions with the amine groups present in GAm hydrogel matrix. The development of Ag NPs in the hydrogel network can be confirmed by the color change of the hydrogel, from pale yellow to dark brown or black. Interestingly, Gils et al. also observed dark brown color change after formation of Ag NPs in their semi -IPN system consists of gum arabic and crosslinked copolymer of poly (2-hydroxymethyl methacrylate-co-acrylic acid) [32].

Fig. 2 depicts the schematic illustration of the formation C. dots embedded GAm hydrogel nanocomposite. In these hydrogel nanocomposites, the hydrophilic groups present on the surface of C. dots interacts with the hydrophilic groups of the polymer matrix.

The formation of Ag NPs in GAm was established by UV-Vis spectroscopy. Fig. 3a show a distinct peak of Ag NPs incorporated within the hydrogel matrix at 435 nm due to surface plasmon resonance effect of Ag NPs in the matrix. Whereas, such a peak was not observed in case of AgNO3 solution. The optical properties of C. dots were also studied using UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The spectra obtained are shown in Fig. 3b. The aqueous solution of C. dots exhibited an absorption peak at 350 nm, and a distinct emission peak at 410 nm when excited at 350 nm [33].

The synthesis of hydrogel and hydrogel nanocomposite systems were confirmed by FTIR. Fig. 4a shows the FTIR spectra of pure Gelatin B, GAm and GAmAg. Ag NPs loaded GAm (GAmAg) shows similar peaks as GAm, with lower intensity and shifts which indicates the interaction of Ag NPs with the hydrophilic groups of GAm hydrogel matrix. It is perceived that the intensity of peak at 1643 cm−1 of GAm corresponding to amide I is reduced in GAmAg. Also, peak at 3188 cm−1 of GAm attributing to -OH stretching and N-H stretching is shifted to 3202 cm−1 with a lower intensity. This confirms successful formation of Ag NPs within the GAm hydrogel matrix [34].

Fig. 4b displays FTIR spectra of synthesized C. dots and C. dots loaded GAm (GAmC). The FTIR spectrum of synthesized C. dots shows a peak at 1707 cm−1 corresponding to CO stretching frequency of carboxylic group [35]. A broad peak ranging from 2818 cm−1 to 3138 cm−1 is due to O-H stretching of carboxylic group. Another peak observed at 1391 cm−1 corresponds to -OH bending of carboxylic acid [36]. While, peak at 1163 cm−1 and 3013 cm−1 is assigned to C-NH-C and N-H stretching. Moreover, small peaks in region from 1000 to 1400 cm−1 are assigned to stretching frequencies of C-O, C-S, and C-H respectively [37]. The intensity of peaks at 1643 cm−1 and 3188 cm−1 of GAm are lowered in GAmC which is due to the interaction of C. dots with the hydrophilic groups of hydrogel matrix. This confirms the incorporation of C. dots in the hydrogel matrix.

The thermal study of GAmAg and GAmC hydrogel nanocomposite was carried out using thermogravimetric analysis. Fig. S1a, show the thermogram of plain GAm and GAmAg. A slight weight loss in the temperature range from 45 °C to 170 °C, due to elimination of moisture from the samples was observed in the first step. Both GAm and GAmAg exhibited higher weight loss in the second step in the temperature range from 225° to 250°C due to degradation of polymer. Also, it is illustrated that the degradation of GAm starts earlier than GAmAg. The end temperature of maximum weight loss was observed at 433 °C for GAm and 450 °C for GAmAg. This shows that incorporation of Ag NPs has increased the thermal stability of GAm. The weights remaining were found to be 37.8% and 44.1% in GAm and GAmAg respectively. The difference between the weights remaining was 6.3%, which reveals the amount of Ag NPs in the system. This method has been adopted by many researchers to determine the amount of nanoparticle in the system [38–40].

Fig. S1b show the thermogram of GAm and GAmC. It was observed that GAmC also followed a similar two step degradation pattern. The first step takes place between 65 °C and 250 °C, due to the loss of adsorbed water, while the second step occurs between 270 °C and 455 °C. The TGA curve of GAmC exhibits lesser weight loss compared to GAm, revealing greater stability of GAmC hydrogel nanocomposite. This could be due to extensive crosslinking within the hydrogel because of the presence of C. dots. Comparable improvement in thermal stability was observed for the C. dots embedded chitosan/carboxymethyl cellulose hydrogel nanocomposite developed by Singh et al. [22]. Such observations were also reported by Konwar et al. [41].

Further, the change in surface morphology before and after the formation of Ag NPs within the GAm hydrogel matrix is shown in Fig. 5a and b. This figure shows successful formation of spherical Ag NPs, distributed within the hydrogel matrix. It is also observed that the Ag NPs are well dispersed without forming aggregates. This could be due to the stabilization of the nanoparticles by the carboxylate ions present on gelatin backbone [42]. The average particle size of Ag NPs determined by SEM analysis was found to be 43 nm. Whereas, the successful incorporation of C. dots in GAm is evident from Fig. 5c and d. In addition, the surface morphology is slightly altered due to the presence of C. dots making the hydrogel more porous.

While the morphology of C. dots was studied using TEM. The TEM image in Fig. S2 reveal the formation of spherical shape and monodispersed C. dots, while the size distribution was found to be between 2 and 5 nm with average size of 3 nm. In Fig. 7b, HRTEM shows the magnified image of C. dots with distinct lattice fringes, which indicates that they are amorphous.

Swelling is a vital parameter of hydrogel with respect to biological aspects as swollen hydrogel mimics a living tissue. The swelling process also plays an important role in drug release and degradation of the hydrogel matrix. It is also well known that incorporation of nanoparticles in the hydrogel system can have great influence on swelling. Hence, the effect of Ag NPs and C. dots present in the GAm hydrogel matrix towards percent equilibrium swelling ratio of hydrogel was studied at different pH conditions such as pH 1.2, pH 6.8 and pH 7.4 at 37 ± 2 °C.
The % ESR at different concentration of AgNO3 is shown in Fig. 6a. On comparing the % ESR of GAm and A1 (5 mM AgNO3), it was observed that there has been an appreciable change in % ESR.

While, on further addition of AgNO3 the increase in % ESR was not found to be much substantial. The Ag NPs formed in the polymer network could increase the number of pores and free space, making the hydrogel network more permeable to water, which results in a higher % ESR. Also, presence of negative surface charge of Ag NPs, leads to generation of ion osmotic pressure which is counter balanced by allowing more water to penetrate in to the system. Likewise, during the formation of Ag NPs, there can be a possibility of breaking of crosslinks which decreases the crosslinking density thereby increasing the % ESR [32]. Similar observations were also noted by Gils et al. [32] and Vimala et al. [43]. Gils et al. had developed a gum arabic based semi-IPN superabsorbent network loaded with nanoparticle within the hydrogel networks through in situ reduction of AgNO3 using trisodium citrate. Likewise, Vimala et al. developed a semi-IPN hydrogel network made of polyacrylamide with different carbohydrate systems like carboxymethylcellulose, starch and gum acacia. In both the studies, formation of Ag NPs within the systems led to increase in swelling capacity. This pattern of swelling was associated with the increase in total porosity of the system, due to the formation of silver nanoparticles inside the network/throughout the gel networks, thereby allowing more water molecules to enter the gel. Also, it could be due to the varied sizes and surface charges of particles formed in the gel networks, causing absolute network expansion. However, the increase in swelling ratio with increase in concentration of AgNO3, is not very significant in the system which could be owing to the knot tying behavior of Ag NPs which restricts the expansion of hydrogel system.

Similarly, the effect of amount of C. dots on % ESR was studied at different pH and is represented in Fig. 6b. From the figure, it is clear that with increase in amount of C. dots, % ESR increases initially, due to the occurrence of more hydrophilic groups on the surface of C. dots (-COOH, -OH, NH2) which causes electrostatic repulsion. This results in expansion of hydrogel matrix and thus allows more diffusion of water into the system. On other hand, with further increase in concentration of C. dots, % ESR decreases. This decrease could be attributed to the additional crosslinks formed between the hydrophilic groups of C. dot and hydrogel. Such additional crosslinking increases the crosslinking density thereby reducing the % ESR.

In this case, maximum swelling was observed at pH 7.4. At this pH, the carboxylic groups on gelatin undergo deprotonation to form carboxylate ions. These carboxylate ions cause electrostatic repulsion, as a result of this; the hydrogel matrix expands and causes higher swelling. Additionally, incorporation of C. dots also could be another reason of improved swelling. This is due to anion-anion repulsion between the hydrophilic groups (-COOH, -OH) present on the surface of C. dots and hydrophilic group within the hydrogel matrix. This leads to expanded network thereby allowing more water infiltration [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading (% DL) and drug entrapment (% DE)
ce:section.ce:para: The drug loading and drug entrapment efficiency of different systems with varying concentration of AgNO3 is revealed in Table S1. As the concentration of AgNO3 increased, the % DL and % DE increased due to the knot-tying behavior of Ag NPs.

While Table S2 exhibits % DL and % DE values for systems with varying concentration of C. dots. It is observed that with an increase in amount of C. dots, the % DL and % DE increases first and later decreases. As the amount of C. dots increases from 0.2 to 0.6 mg mL−1 the hydrophilic interactions of C. dots with GAmC increases gradually. As a result, % DL and % DE increases initially. However, when the amount of C. dots is increased to 1.0 mg mL−1, the % DL and % DE is decreased owing to the highly compact nature of GAm caused by additional crosslinks due to C. dots.

The effect of Ag NPs and C. dots on the CFX release from GAm hydrogel matrix was investigated. In vitro CFX release study from various systems of GAmAg and GAmC was carried out in different pH like pH 1.2, pH 6.8 and pH 7.4 at 37 ± 2 °C for 8 h.

Fig. 7a represents the effect of concentration of AgNO3used for synthesis of Ag NPs on % cumulative drug release under different pH conditions studied at 37 ± 2 °C for 8 h. It has been observed that with the increase in concentration of AgNO3 (i.e. 0 mM, 5 mM, 10 mM and 15 mM represented as GAm, A1, A2 and A3 respectively) the drug release has become more controlled.

This release behavior of CFX from GAmAg can be explained as follows. CFX has to follow a longer path to get released into the external medium in case of GAmAg as compared to GAm. Also, there is a possibility of strong interaction between CFX and Ag NPs through chelation within the hydrogel matrix. Due to these reasons, CFX release from the nanocomposite system is more controlled. It is also observed that CFX release is more at pH 7.4. At pH 7.4, the carboxylic group, present in gelatin, dissociates to negatively charged carboxylate ions. Also, partial hydrolysis of amide group of polyacrylamide chain increases the carboxylate groups in the hydrogel matrix. Thus, higher electrostatic repulsion among the carboxylate ions causes the hydrogel matrix to expand and allows penetration of more water. This increases the swelling and consequently increases the drug release. Likewise, Fig. 7b reveals the effect C.dot on the CFX release. The drug release depends on the pH of the solution and the crosslinking density.

It was observed that with an increase in amount of C. dots, the CFX release was more controlled. As the amount of C. dots increases, the physical crosslinks in GAmC increases resulting in a dense and compact hydrogel network which hinders the drug release irrespective of the pH. From this figure, it was also understood that maximum release of drug took place at pH 7.4 as compared to pH 1.2 [44,45]. Controlled release of CFX is achieved mainly due to two reasons. Firstly, incorporation of C. dots results in formation of more compact and denser hydrogel matrix and secondly, C. dots and hydrogel matrix forms a double drug carrier system resulting in controlled release of drug [46].

The drug release kinetics of GAmAg and GAmC was investigated by fitting the in vitro drug release data with various mathematical models such as zero order kinetics, first order kinetics, Higuchi model and Korsmeyer Peppas model. The regression coefficient values obtained from these models is presented in tabular form in Table S3. The model with R2 values closer to one are considered suitable for the system to follow. From the data, it is apparent that different systems of GAmAg and GAmC have zero order kinetics, which implies that the drug is released regardless of its concentration. The release exponent values “n”, obtained from Peppas model were larger than 0.89 suggesting that the systems followed Super case II transport mechanism.

The biodegradation studies for GAmAg and GAmC were carried out by weight loss method using lysozyme enzyme. Fig. S3a and S3b reveal the biodegradation pattern of GAmAg and GAmC respectively. The gradual increase in weight loss revealed a controlled enzymatic biodegradation of the hydrogel system over the time period of 30 days. The inset reveals the SEM images taken after 30 days of degradation. The formation of cracks and grooves in the hydrogel matrix indicates the degradation of polymer matrix by lysozyme. During enzymatic degradation initially, the enzyme diffuses within the polymeric matrix from the bulk solution. A complex enzyme substrate is subsequently formed, followed by catalytic hydrolysis. During the process of hydrolysis, the hydrolysable bonds (glycoside, amide, carbonates etc.) show hydrolytic degradation. This results in soluble degraded polymeric byproducts which diffuse out in to the bulk solution from the polymer substrate. This causes decrease in initial weight of the polymer taken for the study [47].

Since Ag NPs are well known to exhibit antibacterial property, a zone of inhibition assay was carried out on GAmAg and GAmC hydrogel nanocomposites against gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) respectively. It was observed from the Fig. 8 that GAm did not show any antibacterial activity against bacterial species.

Whereas, GAmAg exhibited distinct zone of inhibition against both strains of bacteria. This antibacterial action of GAmAg was due to presence of Ag NPs which generates surface charge on the hydrogel. The interaction between the charged surface of hydrogel and bacterial membrane is responsible for the antibacterial activity [48]. This confirms that incorporation of Ag NPs in the GAm hydrogel matrix improves the antibacterial property of hydrogel system. On other hand, GAmC did not show any antibacterial activity against the bacterial strains.

In the present work, GAmAg and GAmC hydrogel nanocomposites were synthesized successfully and characterized. GAmAg and GAmC revealed enhanced swelling behavior as compared to plain GAm depending on the content of Ag NPs and C. dots. It also showed improved drug loading and drug entrapment efficiency. GAmAg exhibited positive results for antibacterial test carried out on gram positive and gram-negative bacterial strains as compared to plain GAm. This confirms that incorporation of Ag NPs added antibacterial property to the hydrogel system. In vitro CFX release studies performed under different pH conditions revealed that in both cases (GAmAg and GAmC) more controlled release was observed. The drug release kinetics demonstrated that both the systems followed zero order kinetics and followed Super case II transport mechanism.

Archana Tanwar: was responsible for Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Pratik Kalode: executed synthesis of carbon dots and supported in discussion of result. Roshni V: executed synthesis of carbon dots and supported in discussion of result. Prema B.K.: executed the antibacterial studies. Pooja Doshi: executed the antibacterial studies. Divya Ottoor: was responsible for Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The procedures followed for studying equilibrium swelling ratio (% ESR), drug loading (DL), drug entrapment (DE), in vitro drug release study, In vitro biodegradation study, antibacterial studies and release kinetics are discussed in the supporting information. Fig. S1: TGA of GAm and a) GAmAg b) GAmC, Fig. S2: a) TEM image of C. dots and b) HRTEM of C. dots, Table S1: % DL and % DE values for systems with varying concentration of AgNO3. Fig S3: Biodegradation study of a) GAmAg (inset: SEM image of degraded GAmAg after 30 days) and b) GAmC (inset: SEM image of degraded GAmC after 30 days). Table S2: % DL and % DE values for systems with varying concentration of C. dots and Table S3: Regression coefficient values (R2) obtained from different kinetic models for GAm, GAmAg and GAmC. Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2023.105922.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
